slides.title: The strength that *fades first* isn't raw power.
slides.sub: Rate of force development is how fast you can produce force. Training for speed may help you stay independent.

slides.title: Speed of force, not just size
slides.body: Rate of force development is *how fast you can apply strength*. It is the difference between standing up easily and struggling out of a chair.

slides.title: Power declines faster
slides.body: Explosive power *fades sooner than raw strength* as we age. The ability to move quickly slips away years before the ability to lift heavy.

slides.title: A signal for independence
slides.body: Lower-body power is linked to *staying mobile later in life*: rising from a chair, climbing stairs, catching yourself before a fall.

slides.title: Move with intent
slides.body: Lift the weight *with real speed*, then lower it under control. A few brisk, explosive reps can support power more than slow grinding.

slides.title: Small bursts, often
slides.body: Quick step-ups, brisk sit-to-stands, light jumps. Two short sessions a week *can build the speed* that protects your future self.

slides.title: Train the speed that *keeps you free*.
slides.body: Vibe Health tracks your movement, steps, sleep and food, and turns them into one daily Health Score so explosive training becomes a habit you keep.
slides.cta_text: search ‘Vibe Health’ on the App Store

caption:
Raw strength gets the attention, but the power to move fast is what fades first, and it quietly decides how independent you stay. 🌿

Explosive power declines sooner than strength, it is linked to mobility later in life, and short bursts of fast, intentional movement can help you keep it. Save this. 🔖

Vibe Health turns movement, sleep and food into one daily score → link in bio.
youtube_title: The Strength That Fades First (and How to Keep It)
